title: Needle-felted cat
subtitle: A sitting tabby cat in marled grey-brown merino with surface-needled tabby stripes, 10 cm tall
excerpt: A sitting tabby cat needle-felted in marled grey-brown merino, approximately 10 cm tall. Darker tabby stripes are worked into the surface by stabbing thin lines of charcoal merino after the base form is complete. The tail is made separately and attached curled around the front feet.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from contemporary needle-felting practice. The tabby stripe technique follows the standard layered-stab approach common to modern needle-felting guilds, where a darker-coloured merino is surface-needled over a compacted base to create the stripe pattern.

glossaryTerms.definition: A needle-felting technique in which a thin layer of contrasting-coloured fibre is applied to the outer surface of an already-compacted base form and stabbed in with shallow, precise strokes. The contrast fibre stays near the surface rather than being driven deep, creating visible colour patterns such as stripes, spots, or shading. Surface felting is distinct from building a base shape: the base must be fully compacted before surface work begins.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: A sitting cat in needle-felted merino is built in two main stages: the base form (body, head, ears, and tail as separate parts, then joined) and the
surface felting

of the tabby stripes. The base is worked in a marled grey-brown merino: a blend of warm grey and brown that reads as the typical tabby ground colour. Charcoal merino is then applied over the surface in thin lines to create the stripe pattern. The sitting position means only the body, head, and wrapped tail need to be constructed as separate pieces; the legs are implied by the seated pose rather than built individually. The finished figure stands approximately 10 cm from base to ear tip.
What you need

items.name: Marled grey-brown merino roving (warm grey and brown blend)
White or pale cream merino roving (chin and chest patch)
Pink merino roving (inner ears and nose)
Felting needle, 38-gauge (medium, for joining and shaping)
Felting needle, 40-gauge (fine, for surface stripes and detail)
High-density foam felting mat, at least 10 cm deep
Palm guard or folded kitchen sponge

content.text: Method
Step 1: Build the body

A seated cat body is roughly cylindrical with a slightly broader base where the haunches sit. Take a large clump of grey-brown merino and roll it into a cylinder about 6 cm tall and 4 cm wide at the base, narrowing slightly toward the top. Place it on the foam mat and stab with the 36-gauge needle, rotating steadily. The base should be flat enough to sit stable on the mat. Work for 8-10 minutes until the cylinder is firm throughout. Check by pressing the sides: the form should not dent under firm finger pressure. Set aside and make the tail before joining any further parts.
Step 2: Make and attach the tail

The tail is built as a separate cylinder and attached before the head so it can be positioned underneath the head's attachment point. Roll a length of grey-brown merino into a cylinder about 7 cm long and 1 cm in diameter. Stab on the mat until firm but not fully rigid: the tail needs to be flexible enough to curve around the front base of the body. Attach one end of the tail to the lower-right side of the body base, using the 38-gauge needle to stab through the tail end into the body. Curve the tail around the front of the body and tuck the tip back in at the lower-left side, stabbing the tip into the body to secure it. The tail should form a smooth curve around the front feet area of the body base.
Step 3: Build and attach the head

Roll a ball of grey-brown merino approximately 3.5 cm in diameter. Stab it on the mat until compact and round. A cat's head is nearly spherical with a very slightly flattened face: stab the front face with 10-15 extra strokes to flatten it slightly. For the ears, pinch two small triangular tufts of grey-brown merino and stab each one lightly on the mat to give it some firmness, leaving the base loose. Attach the ears to the top of the head by stabbing the loose base into the head with the 38-gauge needle, positioning them at the two upper corners of the face. Add a tiny wisp of pink merino inside each ear and stab it lightly. Join the head to the top of the body by holding it in position and stabbing through the join from all sides with the 38-gauge needle. The head should not wobble.
Step 4: Cover and smooth the surface

Switch to the 38-gauge needle and cover any joins or rough patches with thin wisps of grey-brown merino, stabbing them smooth. Add the white chin and chest patch at this stage: lay a wisp of white or pale cream merino over the chin and upper chest area and stab it in with the 40-gauge needle using shallow strokes. The white patch should taper from the chin down into the chest. Add a tiny pinch of pink merino for the nose, positioned in the centre of the face just below the midpoint. Stab the nose in with the 40-gauge needle using 4-5 precise strokes. Use the 40-gauge needle over the entire surface for a final smoothing pass.
Step 5: Add the tabby stripes

The surface must be fully compacted and even before adding stripes. Pull a very thin wisp of charcoal merino and lay it in a narrow line across the back of the body, running from the shoulder area down toward the rump. Use the 40-gauge needle to stab along the line with short, shallow strokes spaced about 2-3 mm apart. The goal is to push the charcoal fibres into the top 1-2 mm of the surface only, not deep into the body. Lay in 3-4 parallel lines across the back, spacing them roughly 6-8 mm apart. Add a single stripe or two along the top of the head, running from ear to ear across the crown. Add one or two faint stripes on each side of the face, radiating outward from the nose area. Press the striped areas with a fingernail after needling: the stripes should feel flush with the surface, not raised.
Step 6: Final check and detail

Stand the cat on a flat surface and check that it sits upright without leaning. Use the 40-gauge needle to refine any areas that look uneven. Check that the tail is firmly attached at both ends and follows a smooth curve. The finished cat should measure approximately 10 cm from the base to the ear tips. If the ears have splayed outward, add a small wisp of grey-brown merino to the outer base of each ear and stab it in to push the ear back to a more upright angle.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The tabby stripes bleed or disappear into the surface
items.cause: The base was not fully compacted before adding stripes, or the needle strokes were too deep
items.fix: Always complete the full base-shaping and smoothing pass before adding any surface colour. Use the 40-gauge needle for all stripe work and keep strokes very shallow. If a stripe has disappeared, add a fresh thin wisp in the same position and needle it in with even shallower strokes.

items.problem: The head is not centred and the cat looks sideways
items.cause: The head was attached at an angle
items.fix: Before fully committing to the join, hold the head in position and view the figure from the front to check the alignment. Once attached, an off-centre head can be corrected by adding extra merino to the lower side of the neck join and stabbing to push the head back toward centre.

items.problem: The tail separates from the body at one attachment point
items.cause: Insufficient stabbing at the tail tip or tail root
items.fix: Lay a small wisp of grey-brown merino over the detached point and stab firmly through both the wisp and the original join. Use the 38-gauge needle and at least 15 strokes at each attachment point to secure the tail properly.

content.text: Care and finishing

Brush loose surface fibres away with a dry soft-bristle brush. Do not machine-wash the figure. If the tabby stripes pick up lint or look dull over time, a light brush will restore the surface. Store away from direct sunlight and in a sealed container or cloth bag to protect against wool moth damage.
